# Being and Becoming

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> Today you'll learn how to talk about professions, past roles, and future goals. Being able to explain what you do, what you used to do, and what you are studying to become is essential for building relationships. By the end of this module, you'll be able to describe your career and aspirations in Ukrainian!

## Вступ (Introduction)
<!-- adapted from: Vashulenko, Grade 3, вправа 54 -->

Welcome to a very important milestone in your language journey! When you meet someone new in Ukraine, one of the first things they will ask you is about your profession. In English, we say "I am a doctor" or "I want to be a doctor." In both of these sentences, the word "doctor" stays exactly the same. However, in Ukrainian, a profession can represent two different grammatical ideas: **identity** or **function**.

When you state your permanent, present identity, you use the **називний відмінок** (nominative case). Think of this as who you are right now. This is a simple statement of fact.

- **Я лікар.** — I am a doctor.
- **Він програміст.** — He is a programmer.
- **Вона вчителька.** — She is a teacher.
- **Ми студенти.** — We are students.
- **Моя сестра лікарка.** — My sister is a doctor.
- **Твоя мама інженерка.** — Your mom is an engineer.

However, when you talk about a temporary role, a past job, or a future career goal, the Ukrainian language shifts. It uses the **орудний відмінок** (instrumental case). This case shows a process, a professional function, or a state of change rather than a fixed, permanent identity. This aligns directly with the official Ukrainian State Standard for describing a person's characteristics by profession or status.

- **Я буду лікарем.** — I will be a doctor.
- **Він хоче стати програмістом.** — He wants to become a programmer.
- **Вона працювала вчителькою.** — She worked as a teacher.
- **Ми були студентами.** — We were students.
- **Моя сестра буде лікаркою.** — My sister will be a doctor.
- **Твоя мама працювала інженеркою.** — Your mom worked as an engineer.

> [!warning] The Nominative Trap
>
> English speakers often fall into a common grammatical trap. Because English uses the same form for present and future professions, learners often want to say: ~~Він хоче бути лікар.~~ This is a direct translation from "He wants to be a doctor," but it sounds very unnatural to a Ukrainian ear! The verb **бути** (to be) in the past or future requires the instrumental case for professions. It must be: **Він хоче бути лікарем.**

Let's practice reading some short texts combining both of these forms. Notice how the endings change when we switch from present identity to past or future roles.

> **(Читання / Reading Practice)**
>
> Мене звати Антон. Зараз я студент, але я дуже люблю комп'ютери. Я хочу бути програмістом. Мій брат працює айтівцем у Києві. Раніше він теж був студентом. Наша сестра — лікарка. Вона довго була лише студенткою, а тепер працює лікаркою в лікарні. Це дуже цікаво!
>
> *(My name is Anton. Right now I am a student, but I really like computers. I want to be a programmer. My brother works as an IT professional in Kyiv. Previously he was also a student. Our sister is a doctor. She was just a student for a long time, and now she works as a doctor in a hospital. It is very interesting!)*

## Презентація: Дієслова та відмінювання (Presentation: Verbs and Governing)
<!-- adapted from: Kravtsova, Grade 4, вправа 53 -->

Now that you understand the logic behind the cases, let's look closely at the three main verbs we use to talk about our careers: **бути** (to be), **стати / ставати** (to become), and **працювати** (to work). All three of these verbs govern the **орудний відмінок** (instrumental case). This means that any profession following these verbs must take an instrumental ending.

First, let's explore the verb **бути**. While the present tense often drops this verb completely (using the nominative case with a null-copula), the past and future tenses absolutely require it, along with the instrumental case. These are some of the most high-frequency collocations you will hear in professional settings.

- **Він був студентом.** — He was a student.
- **Вона була студенткою.** — She was a student.
- **Я буду лікарем.** — I will be a doctor.
- **Ти будеш вчителем?** — Will you be a teacher?
- **Ми були друзями.** — We were friends.
- **Вони будуть інженерами.** — They will be engineers.
- **Ми будемо працювати юристами.** — We will work as lawyers.

Next, we have the verbs for "becoming": the perfective verb **стати** (to become a finished state) and the imperfective verb **ставати** (to be becoming, describing an ongoing process). The verb **стати** is extremely common when discussing career aspirations and future goals. You will often hear the construction **хоче стати...** (wants to become...).

- **Він хоче стати спеціалістом.** — He wants to become a specialist.
- **Вона стала дуже відомою журналісткою.** — She became a very famous journalist.
- **Він довго ставав кращим програмістом.** — He was becoming a better programmer for a long time.
- **Вона довго ставала хорошою вчителькою.** — She was becoming a good teacher for a long time.
- **Кожного дня ти повинен ставати кращим.** — Every day you must be becoming better.
- **Я буду хорошим юристом.** — I will be a good lawyer.
- **Ми стали студентами.** — We became students.
- **Вони хочуть стати інженерами.** — They want to become engineers.

Finally, we use the verb **працювати** (to work) to describe our current, past, or future jobs. This verb also requires the instrumental case.

> [!caution] The 'Work As' Calque
>
> In English, you say "I work *as* a manager." You might be tempted to translate the word "as" into the Ukrainian word **як**. Never say: ~~Він працює як менеджер~~! This is a translation calque. In Ukrainian, the verb **працювати** directly takes the instrumental case with no extra prepositions. You simply say: **Він працює менеджером** (He works "by means of" a manager).

- **Я працюю вчителькою.** — I work as a teacher.
- **Він працює лікарем.** — He works as a doctor.
- **Вона працює менеджеркою.** — She works as a manager.
- **Мій друг працює юристом.** — My friend works as a lawyer.
- **Ти хочеш працювати програмістом?** — Do you want to work as a programmer?

Let's introduce some primary profession vocabulary. Notice how we provide both masculine and feminine forms for each profession, alongside their instrumental endings!

- **лікар / лікарка** → лікарем / лікаркою (doctor)
- **вчитель / вчителька** → вчителем / вчителькою (teacher)
- **інженер / інженерка** → інженером / інженеркою (engineer)
- **журналіст / журналістка** → журналістом / журналісткою (journalist)
- **юрист / юристка** → юристом / юристкою (lawyer)
- **програміст / програмістка** → програмістом / програмісткою (programmer)
- **економіст / економістка** → економістом / економісткою (economist)

When discussing technology roles, you might encounter the formal word **програмувальник**. While this is perfectly correct, the word **програміст** is far more universally used in everyday speech and professional environments. Both require the instrumental case.

> **(Читання / Reading Practice)**
>
> Моя мама все життя працювала вчителькою в школі. Вона дуже любить дітей і свою роботу. Мій батько працює інженером на великому заводі. А я хочу стати журналісткою. Я вже працюю в газеті, але зараз я тільки студентка. Минулого року мій старший брат став юристом. Він працює в центрі міста.
>
> *(My mom worked as a teacher in a school her whole life. She loves children and her job very much. My father works as an engineer at a big factory. And I want to become a journalist. I already work at a newspaper, but right now I am only a student. Last year my older brother became a lawyer. He works in the city center.)*

## Соціокультурний контекст: Фемінітиви та IT (Sociocultural Context: Femininitives and IT)
<!-- adapted from: Savchenko, Grade 4, вправа 93 -->

When learning Ukrainian vocabulary for professions, it is absolutely vital to understand two major modern cultural trends: the widespread, official use of femininitives and the incredibly high prestige of the IT industry.

In 2020, the Ukrainian government passed a major Grammar Reform. For many decades, the Russian language strongly influenced Ukrainian grammar rules, making masculine forms the default for almost all official professions. The 2020 reform officially codified femininitives — words that specifically identify a female professional — returning the language to its natural, historic roots. In the past, words like **директор** (director) or **менеджер** (manager) were often used for both men and women in formal contexts. Today, using the feminine forms reflects modern societal shifts towards gender equality and respect.

- **Він хороший директор.** — He is a good director.
- **Вона дуже хороша директорка.** — She is a very good director.
- **Цей чоловік — мій менеджер.** — This man is my manager.
- **Ця жінка — моя менеджерка.** — This woman is my manager.
- **Він відомий філолог.** — He is a famous philologist.
- **Вона відома філологиня.** — She is a famous philologist.

You should practice using both masculine and feminine forms consistently. It is a clear sign of an educated, contemporary Ukrainian speaker! Using words like **директорка** and **менеджерка** is not just grammatically correct; it is culturally expected.

Another major aspect of modern Ukraine is the booming technology sector. Ukraine is known globally as a massive European IT hub, and careers in tech are highly sought after by young people. Because of this high prestige, you will often hear two different words for a tech professional. The formal word is **програміст** (or **програмістка**). However, in everyday conversation, almost everyone uses the colloquial term **айтівець** (IT professional, masculine) and **айтівка** (IT professional, feminine).

> [!culture] A Modern Dream
>
> It is a common cultural observation in Ukraine that almost every young person wants to join the IT sector because of its stability and international connections. It is very common to hear someone joke: **«Кожен другий хоче стати айтівцем!»** (Every second person wants to become an IT professional!).

- **Мій син хоче стати програмістом.** — My son wants to become a programmer.
- **Її донька працює айтівкою.** — Her daughter works as an IT professional.
- **Він працює успішним айтівцем.** — He works as a successful IT professional.

> **(Читання / Reading Practice)**
>
> Сучасна Україна дуже швидко змінюється. Сьогодні багато жінок працюють директорками та менеджерками. Також дуже популярна сфера ІТ. Кожен другий студент хоче працювати айтівцем або програмістом. Моя подруга Олена стала дуже хорошою програмісткою. Вона працює айтівкою у великій міжнародній компанії. Її мама теж багато працює — вона працює директоркою школи.
>
> *(Modern Ukraine is changing very quickly. Today many women work as directors and managers. The IT sphere is also very popular. Every second student wants to work as an IT professional or a programmer. My friend Olena became a very good programmer. She works as an IT professional in a large international company. Her mom also works a lot — she works as a school director.)*

## Практика та запобігання помилкам (Practice and Error Prevention)
<!-- adapted from: Varzatska, Grade 4, вправа 237 -->

Let's actively practice transforming present identities into professional roles and work on correcting common learner mistakes! 

When changing a present-tense identity into a past or future role, you must change the ending of the noun to the **орудний відмінок** (instrumental case). Watch carefully how the endings change for masculine nouns (usually adding **-ом** or **-ем**) and feminine nouns (usually adding **-ою** or **-ею**).

- **Я економіст.** → **Я був економістом.** (I was an economist.)
- **Ти юристка.** → **Ти будеш юристкою.** (You will be a lawyer.)
- **Він журналіст.** → **Він став журналістом.** (He became a journalist.)
- **Вона інженерка.** → **Вона стала головною інженеркою.** (She became a chief engineer.)
- **Я спеціаліст.** → **Я хочу стати хорошим спеціалістом.** (I want to become a good specialist.)
- **Ти лікар.** → **Ти будеш працювати лікарем.** (You will work as a doctor.)

> [!tip] Gender Mismatch Warning
>
> When talking about a woman's profession, you must ensure that both the adjective and the noun match her gender! English speakers sometimes default to the masculine form because English does not have gendered professions. Avoid saying: ~~Вона хороший лікар.~~
> Instead, correctly drill the agreement between the gendered person noun and the profession: **Вона хороша лікарка.**

Let's drill some gender-matching phrases. Note how the instrumental case affects both the adjective and the noun simultaneously!

- **Він був хорошим лікарем.** — He was a good doctor.
- **Вона була дуже хорошою лікаркою.** — She was a very good doctor.
- **Він хоче стати новим директором.** — He wants to become the new director.
- **Вона хоче стати новою директоркою.** — She wants to become the new director.
- **Він працює головним інженером.** — He works as a chief engineer.
- **Вона працює головною інженеркою.** — She works as a chief engineer.

Finally, we must practice removing the translation calque when using the verb **працювати** (to work). Exercises specifically designed to root out the use of the word **як** (as) are crucial for sounding natural. Remember, the instrumental case alone provides the meaning of "as."

- ~~Він працює як економіст.~~ → **Він працює економістом.** (He works as an economist.)
- ~~Вона працює як спеціаліст.~~ → **Вона працює спеціалісткою.** (She works as a specialist.)
- ~~Я працював як журналіст.~~ → **Я працював журналістом.** (I worked as a journalist.)

> **(Читання / Reading Practice)**
>
> Раніше мій дідусь був інженером. Він багато років працював головним інженером на великому заводі. Моя бабуся була дуже відомою економісткою. Вона ніколи не працювала як журналістка чи вчителька. Вона завжди працювала економісткою в банку. Зараз я студент, але я мрію стати відомим спеціалістом. Моя молодша сестра хоче стати юристкою.
>
> *(Previously my grandpa was an engineer. He worked as a chief engineer at a big factory for many years. My grandma was a famous economist. She never worked as a journalist or a teacher. She always worked as an economist in a bank. Right now I am a student, but I dream of becoming a famous specialist. My younger sister wants to become a lawyer.)*

## Діалоги та кар'єрні плани (Dialogues and Career Plans)
<!-- adapted from: Kravtsova, Grade 3, вправа 313 -->

The most common way to ask someone about their job in Ukrainian is **«Ким ви працюєте?»** (Who do you work as?) in formal situations, or **«Ким ти працюєш?»** in informal situations. Notice that the question word **хто** (who) changes to its instrumental form **ким** (by whom). 

Let's look at some natural conversations about careers, past roles, and future goals. These dialogues model natural modern usage in IT and professional contexts. Read them aloud to practice your pronunciation!

> **(В офісі / In the office)**
> — Добрий день! Ви тут новий співробітник? Ким ви працюєте?
> — Добрий день! Так. Я працюю менеджером. А ви?
> — Дуже приємно! А я працюю головною директоркою тут.
> — О, це цікаво. Я раніше працював інженером.
>
> *(— Good afternoon! Are you a new employee? Who do you work as?)*
> *(— Good afternoon! Yes. I work as a manager. And you?)*
> *(— Nice to meet you! And I work as the chief director here.)*
> *(— Oh, that is interesting. I previously worked as an engineer.)*

> **(На вулиці / On the street)**
> — Привіт, Олеже! Давно не бачилися! Ким ти зараз працюєш?
> — Привіт! Я став програмістом. Працюю айтівцем у новій компанії.
> — Клас! А я ще студент. Хочу стати юристом.
> — Бажаю успіху! Це хороша професія.
>
> *(— Hi, Oleh! Long time no see! Who do you work as now?)*
> *(— Hi! I became a programmer. I work as an IT professional in a new company.)*
> *(— Cool! And I am still a student. I want to become a lawyer.)*
> *(— I wish you success! It is a good profession.)*

We also use these exact same verbs and cases to talk about citizenship and broader life aspirations. In the official Ukrainian State Standard for language learning, there is a beautiful example sentence that reflects the goals of many people learning the language today: **Вона мріє стати громадянкою України.** (She dreams of becoming a citizen of Ukraine.)

- **Він став громадянином.** — He became a citizen.
- **Вона хоче стати громадянкою.** — She wants to become a citizen.
- **Я мрію бути громадянином України.** — I dream of being a citizen of Ukraine.
- **Ти будеш хорошим громадянином.** — You will be a good citizen.

> [!did-you-know] Synthesis: Your Career Story
>
> You can now describe your entire career path! Practice writing down your own story using the full range of vocabulary introduced today: write what you were in the past (**був / була**), what you work as now (**працюю**), and what you want to become in the future (**хочу стати**). 

> **(Читання / Reading Practice)**
>
> Це Анна. Раніше вона жила в іншій країні і була простою офіціанткою. Вона багато працювала, але дуже хотіла змінити своє життя. Вона багато вчилася і стала програмісткою. Тепер вона працює айтівкою в Києві. Вона дуже любить Україну. Вона мріє стати громадянкою України наступного року. Її чоловік теж хоче стати громадянином.
>
> *(This is Anna. Previously she lived in another country and was a simple waitress. She worked a lot, but really wanted to change her life. She studied a lot and became a programmer. Now she works as an IT professional in Kyiv. She loves Ukraine very much. She dreams of becoming a citizen of Ukraine next year. Her husband also wants to become a citizen.)*

---

# Підсумок

Congratulations! You can now confidently describe your career path and professional aspirations in Ukrainian! 

You learned that while present-tense identities use the nominative case (**Я лікар**), your past roles, future goals, and current functions all require the instrumental case (**Я був лікарем**, **Я працюю лікарем**). You also explored the modern, respectful use of femininitives and essential IT vocabulary that reflects contemporary Ukrainian society. 

Here are a few questions to check your knowledge:

1. **Як сказати українською: "I want to become a programmer"?**
   *(How do you say in Ukrainian: "I want to become a programmer"?)*
   → Я хочу стати програмістом (або програмісткою).

2. **Яка правильна форма: "Він працює як менеджер" чи "Він працює менеджером"?**
   *(Which is the correct form: "Він працює як менеджер" or "Він працює менеджером"?)*
   → Він працює менеджером.

3. **Як змінити речення "Вона лікар" на минулий час?**
   *(How do you change the sentence "Вона лікар" to the past tense?)*
   → Вона була лікаркою.

4. **Як сказати українською: "She dreams of becoming a citizen of Ukraine"?**
   *(How do you say in Ukrainian: "She dreams of becoming a citizen of Ukraine"?)*
   → Вона мріє стати громадянкою України.

---

toolCalls.description:
Replaces text within a file. By default, the tool expects to find and replace exactly ONE occurrence of `old_string`. If you want to replace multiple occurrences of the exact same string, set `allow_multiple` to true. This tool requires providing significant context around the change to ensure precise targeting. Always use the read_file tool to examine the file's current content before attempting a text replacement.
      
      The user has the ability to modify the `new_string` content. If modified, this will be stated in the response.
      
      Expectation for required parameters:
      1. `old_string` MUST be the exact literal text to replace (including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.).
      2. `new_string` MUST be the exact literal text to replace `old_string` with (also including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.). Ensure the resulting code is correct and idiomatic and that `old_string` and `new_string` are different.
      3. `instruction` is the detailed instruction of what needs to be changed. It is important to Make it specific and detailed so developers or large language models can understand what needs to be changed and perform the changes on their own if necessary. 
      4. NEVER escape `old_string` or `new_string`, that would break the exact literal text requirement.
      **Important:** If ANY of the above are not satisfied, the tool will fail. CRITICAL for `old_string`: Must uniquely identify the instance(s) to change. Include at least 3 lines of context BEFORE and AFTER the target text, matching whitespace and indentation precisely. If this string matches multiple locations and `allow_multiple` is not true, the tool will fail.
      5. Prefer to break down complex and long changes into multiple smaller atomic calls to this tool. Always check the content of the file after changes or not finding a string to match.
      **Multiple replacements:** Set `allow_multiple` to true if you want to replace ALL occurrences that match `old_string` exactly.

<!-- SCOPE
Covers: Professions, instrumental case with verbs бути, стати, працювати
Not covered:
  - Complex instrumental prepositions (з, над, під) → a2-07
-->

# Being and Becoming

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> Today you'll learn how to talk about professions, past roles, and future goals. Being able to explain what you do, what you used to do, and what you are studying to become is essential for building relationships. By the end of this module, you'll be able to describe your career and aspirations in Ukrainian!

## Вступ (Introduction)
<!-- adapted from: Vashulenko, Grade 3, вправа 54 -->

Welcome to a very important milestone in your language journey! When you meet someone new in Ukraine, one of the first things they will ask you is about your profession. In English, we say "I am a doctor" or "I want to be a doctor." In both of these sentences, the word "doctor" stays exactly the same. However, in Ukrainian, a profession can represent two different grammatical ideas: **identity** or **function**.

When you state your permanent, present identity, you use the **називний відмінок** (nominative case). Think of this as who you are right now. This is a simple statement of fact.

- **Я лікар.** — I am a doctor.
- **Він програміст.** — He is a programmer.
- **Вона вчителька.** — She is a teacher.
- **Ми студенти.** — We are students.
- **Моя сестра лікарка.** — My sister is a doctor.
- **Твоя мама інженерка.** — Your mom is an engineer.

However, when you talk about a temporary role, a past job, or a future career goal, the Ukrainian language shifts. It uses the **орудний відмінок** (instrumental case). This case shows a process, a professional function, or a state of change rather than a fixed, permanent identity. This aligns directly with the official Ukrainian State Standard for describing a person's characteristics by profession or status.

- **Я буду лікарем.** — I will be a doctor.
- **Він хоче стати програмістом.** — He wants to become a programmer.
- **Вона працювала вчителькою.** — She worked as a teacher.
- **Ми були студентами.** — We were students.
- **Моя сестра буде лікаркою.** — My sister will be a doctor.
- **Твоя мама працювала інженеркою.** — Your mom worked as an engineer.

> [!warning] The Nominative Trap
>
> English speakers often fall into a common grammatical trap. Because English uses the same form for present and future professions, learners often want to say: ~~Він хоче бути лікар.~~ This is a direct translation from "He wants to be a doctor," but it sounds very unnatural to a Ukrainian ear! The verb **бути** (to be) in the past or future requires the instrumental case for professions. It must be: **Він хоче бути лікарем.**

Let's practice reading some short texts combining both of these forms. Notice how the endings change when we switch from present identity to past or future roles.

> **(Читання / Reading Practice)**
>
> Мене звати Антон. Зараз я студент, але я дуже люблю комп'ютери. Я хочу бути програмістом. Мій брат працює айтівцем у Києві. Раніше він теж був студентом. Наша сестра — лікарка. Вона довго була лише студенткою, а тепер працює лікаркою в лікарні. Це дуже цікаво!
>
> *(My name is Anton. Right now I am a student, but I really like computers. I want to be a programmer. My brother works as an IT professional in Kyiv. Previously he was also a student. Our sister is a doctor. She was just a student for a long time, and now she works as a doctor in a hospital. It is very interesting!)*

## Презентація: Дієслова та відмінювання (Presentation: Verbs and Governing)
<!-- adapted from: Kravtsova, Grade 4, вправа 53 -->

Now that you understand the logic behind the cases, let's look closely at the three main verbs we use to talk about our careers: **бути** (to be), **стати / ставати** (to become), and **працювати** (to work). All three of these verbs govern the **орудний відмінок** (instrumental case). This means that any profession following these verbs must take an instrumental ending.

First, let's explore the verb **бути**. While the present tense often drops this verb completely (using the nominative case with a null-copula), the past and future tenses absolutely require it, along with the instrumental case. These are some of the most high-frequency collocations you will hear in professional settings.

- **Він був студентом.** — He was a student.
- **Вона була студенткою.** — She was a student.
- **Я буду лікарем.** — I will be a doctor.
- **Ти будеш вчителем?** — Will you be a teacher?
- **Ми були друзями.** — We were friends.
- **Вони будуть інженерами.** — They will be engineers.
- **Ми будемо працювати юристами.** — We will work as lawyers.

Next, we have the verbs for "becoming": the perfective verb **стати** (to become a finished state) and the imperfective verb **ставати** (to be becoming, describing an ongoing process). The verb **стати** is extremely common when discussing career aspirations and future goals. You will often hear the construction **хоче стати...** (wants to become...).

- **Він хоче стати спеціалістом.** — He wants to become a specialist.
- **Вона стала дуже відомою журналісткою.** — She became a very famous journalist.
- **Він довго ставав кращим програмістом.** — He was becoming a better programmer for a long time.
- **Вона довго ставала хорошою вчителькою.** — She was becoming a good teacher for a long time.
- **Кожного дня ти повинен ставати кращим.** — Every day you must be becoming better.
- **Я буду хорошим юристом.** — I will be a good lawyer.
- **Ми стали студентами.** — We became students.
- **Вони хочуть стати інженерами.** — They want to become engineers.

Finally, we use the verb **працювати** (to work) to describe our current, past, or future jobs. This verb also requires the instrumental case.

> [!caution] The 'Work As' Calque
>
> In English, you say "I work *as* a manager." You might be tempted to translate the word "as" into the Ukrainian word **як**. Never say: ~~Він працює як менеджер~~! This is a translation calque. In Ukrainian, the verb **працювати** directly takes the instrumental case with no extra prepositions. You simply say: **Він працює менеджером** (He works "by means of" a manager).

- **Я працюю вчителькою.** — I work as a teacher.
- **Він працює лікарем.** — He works as a doctor.
- **Вона працює менеджеркою.** — She works as a manager.
- **Мій друг працює юристом.** — My friend works as a lawyer.
- **Ти хочеш працювати програмістом?** — Do you want to work as a programmer?

Let's introduce some primary profession vocabulary. Notice how we provide both masculine and feminine forms for each profession, alongside their instrumental endings!

- **лікар / лікарка** → лікарем / лікаркою (doctor)
- **вчитель / вчителька** → вчителем / вчителькою (teacher)
- **інженер / інженерка** → інженером / інженеркою (engineer)
- **журналіст / журналістка** → журналістом / журналісткою (journalist)
- **юрист / юристка** → юристом / юристкою (lawyer)
- **програміст / програмістка** → програмістом / програмісткою (programmer)
- **економіст / економістка** → економістом / економісткою (economist)

When discussing technology roles, you might encounter the formal word **програмувальник**. While this is perfectly correct, the word **програміст** is far more universally used in everyday speech and professional environments. Both require the instrumental case.

> **(Читання / Reading Practice)**
>
> Моя мама все життя працювала вчителькою в школі. Вона дуже любить дітей і свою роботу. Мій батько працює інженером на великому заводі. А я хочу стати журналісткою. Я вже працюю в газеті, але зараз я тільки студентка. Минулого року мій старший брат став юристом. Він працює в центрі міста.
>
> *(My mom worked as a teacher in a school her whole life. She loves children and her job very much. My father works as an engineer at a big factory. And I want to become a journalist. I already work at a newspaper, but right now I am only a student. Last year my older brother became a lawyer. He works in the city center.)*

## Соціокультурний контекст: Фемінітиви та IT (Sociocultural Context: Femininitives and IT)
<!-- adapted from: Savchenko, Grade 4, вправа 93 -->

When learning Ukrainian vocabulary for professions, it is absolutely vital to understand two major modern cultural trends: the widespread, official use of femininitives and the incredibly high prestige of the IT industry.

In 2020, the Ukrainian government passed a major Grammar Reform. For many decades, the Russian language strongly influenced Ukrainian grammar rules, making masculine forms the default for almost all official professions. The 2020 reform officially codified femininitives — words that specifically identify a female professional — returning the language to its natural, historic roots. In the past, words like **директор** (director) or **менеджер** (manager) were often used for both men and women in formal contexts. Today, using the feminine forms reflects modern societal shifts towards gender equality and respect.

- **Він хороший директор.** — He is a good director.
- **Вона дуже хороша директорка.** — She is a very good director.
- **Цей чоловік — мій менеджер.** — This man is my manager.
- **Ця жінка — моя менеджерка.** — This woman is my manager.
- **Він відомий філолог.** — He is a famous philologist.
- **Вона відома філологиня.** — She is a famous philologist.

You should practice using both masculine and feminine forms consistently. It is a clear sign of an educated, contemporary Ukrainian speaker! Using words like **директорка** and **менеджерка** is not just grammatically correct; it is culturally expected.

Another major aspect of modern Ukraine is the booming technology sector. Ukraine is known globally as a massive European IT hub, and careers in tech are highly sought after by young people. Because of this high prestige, you will often hear two different words for a tech professional. The formal word is **програміст** (or **програмістка**). However, in everyday conversation, almost everyone uses the colloquial term **айтівець** (IT professional, masculine) and **айтівка** (IT professional, feminine).

> [!culture] A Modern Dream
>
> It is a common cultural observation in Ukraine that almost every young person wants to join the IT sector because of its stability and international connections. It is very common to hear someone joke: **«Кожен другий хоче стати айтівцем!»** (Every second person wants to become an IT professional!).

- **Мій син хоче стати програмістом.** — My son wants to become a programmer.
- **Її донька працює айтівкою.** — Her daughter works as an IT professional.
- **Він працює успішним айтівцем.** — He works as a successful IT professional.

> **(Читання / Reading Practice)**
>
> Сучасна Україна дуже швидко змінюється. Сьогодні багато жінок працюють директорками та менеджерками. Також дуже популярна сфера ІТ. Кожен другий студент хоче працювати айтівцем або програмістом. Моя подруга Олена стала дуже хорошою програмісткою. Вона працює айтівкою у великій міжнародній компанії. Її мама теж багато працює — вона працює директоркою школи.
>
> *(Modern Ukraine is changing very quickly. Today many women work as directors and managers. The IT sphere is also very popular. Every second student wants to work as an IT professional or a programmer. My friend Olena became a very good programmer. She works as an IT professional in a large international company. Her mom also works a lot — she works as a school director.)*

## Практика та запобігання помилкам (Practice and Error Prevention)
<!-- adapted from: Varzatska, Grade 4, вправа 237 -->

Let's actively practice transforming present identities into professional roles and work on correcting common learner mistakes! 

When changing a present-tense identity into a past or future role, you must change the ending of the noun to the **орудний відмінок** (instrumental case). Watch carefully how the endings change for masculine nouns (usually adding **-ом** or **-ем**) and feminine nouns (usually adding **-ою** or **-ею**).

- **Я економіст.** → **Я був економістом.** (I was an economist.)
- **Ти юристка.** → **Ти будеш юристкою.** (You will be a lawyer.)
- **Він журналіст.** → **Він став журналістом.** (He became a journalist.)
- **Вона інженерка.** → **Вона стала головною інженеркою.** (She became a chief engineer.)
- **Я спеціаліст.** → **Я хочу стати хорошим спеціалістом.** (I want to become a good specialist.)
- **Ти лікар.** → **Ти будеш працювати лікарем.** (You will work as a doctor.)

> [!tip] Gender Mismatch Warning
>
> When talking about a woman's profession, you must ensure that both the adjective and the noun match her gender! English speakers sometimes default to the masculine form because English does not have gendered professions. Avoid saying: ~~Вона хороший лікар.~~
> Instead, correctly drill the agreement between the gendered person noun and the profession: **Вона хороша лікарка.**

Let's drill some gender-matching phrases. Note how the instrumental case affects both the adjective and the noun simultaneously!

- **Він був хорошим лікарем.** — He was a good doctor.
- **Вона була дуже хорошою лікаркою.** — She was a very good doctor.
- **Він хоче стати новим директором.** — He wants to become the new director.
- **Вона хоче стати новою директоркою.** — She wants to become the new director.
- **Він працює головним інженером.** — He works as a chief engineer.
- **Вона працює головною інженеркою.** — She works as a chief engineer.

Finally, we must practice removing the translation calque when using the verb **працювати** (to work). Exercises specifically designed to root out the use of the word **як** (as) are crucial for sounding natural. Remember, the instrumental case alone provides the meaning of "as."

- ~~Він працює як економіст.~~ → **Він працює економістом.** (He works as an economist.)
- ~~Вона працює як спеціаліст.~~ → **Вона працює спеціалісткою.** (She works as a specialist.)
- ~~Я працював як журналіст.~~ → **Я працював журналістом.** (I worked as a journalist.)

> **(Читання / Reading Practice)**
>
> Раніше мій дідусь був інженером. Він багато років працював головним інженером на великому заводі. Моя бабуся була дуже відомою економісткою. Вона ніколи не працювала як журналістка чи вчителька. Вона завжди працювала економісткою в банку. Зараз я студент, але я мрію стати відомим спеціалістом. Моя молодша сестра хоче стати юристкою.
>
> *(Previously my grandpa was an engineer. He worked as a chief engineer at a big factory for many years. My grandma was a famous economist. She never worked as a journalist or a teacher. She always worked as an economist in a bank. Right now I am a student, but I dream of becoming a famous specialist. My younger sister wants to become a lawyer.)*

## Діалоги та кар'єрні плани (Dialogues and Career Plans)
<!-- adapted from: Kravtsova, Grade 3, вправа 313 -->

The most common way to ask someone about their job in Ukrainian is **«Ким ви працюєте?»** (Who do you work as?) in formal situations, or **«Ким ти працюєш?»** in informal situations. Notice that the question word **хто** (who) changes to its instrumental form **ким** (by whom). 

Let's look at some natural conversations about careers, past roles, and future goals. These dialogues model natural modern usage in IT and professional contexts. Read them aloud to practice your pronunciation!

> **(В офісі / In the office)**
> — Добрий день! Ви тут новий співробітник? Ким ви працюєте?
> — Добрий день! Так. Я працюю менеджером. А ви?
> — Дуже приємно! А я працюю головною директоркою тут.
> — О, це цікаво. Я раніше працював інженером.
>
> *(— Good afternoon! Are you a new employee? Who do you work as?)*
> *(— Good afternoon! Yes. I work as a manager. And you?)*
> *(— Nice to meet you! And I work as the chief director here.)*
> *(— Oh, that is interesting. I previously worked as an engineer.)*

> **(На вулиці / On the street)**
> — Привіт, Олеже! Давно не бачилися! Ким ти зараз працюєш?
> — Привіт! Я став програмістом. Працюю айтівцем у новій компанії.
> — Клас! А я ще студент. Хочу стати юристом.
> — Бажаю успіху! Це хороша професія.
>
> *(— Hi, Oleh! Long time no see! Who do you work as now?)*
> *(— Hi! I became a programmer. I work as an IT professional in a new company.)*
> *(— Cool! And I am still a student. I want to become a lawyer.)*
> *(— I wish you success! It is a good profession.)*

We also use these exact same verbs and cases to talk about citizenship and broader life aspirations. In the official Ukrainian State Standard for language learning, there is a beautiful example sentence that reflects the goals of many people learning the language today: **Вона мріє стати громадянкою України.** (She dreams of becoming a citizen of Ukraine.)

- **Він став громадянином.** — He became a citizen.
- **Вона хоче стати громадянкою.** — She wants to become a citizen.
- **Я мрію бути громадянином України.** — I dream of being a citizen of Ukraine.
- **Ти будеш хорошим громадянином.** — You will be a good citizen.

> [!did-you-know] Synthesis: Your Career Story
>
> You can now describe your entire career path! Practice writing down your own story using the full range of vocabulary introduced today: write what you were in the past (**був / була**), what you work as now (**працюю**), and what you want to become in the future (**хочу стати**). 

> **(Читання / Reading Practice)**
>
> Це Анна. Раніше вона жила в іншій країні і була простою офіціанткою. Вона багато працювала, але дуже хотіла змінити своє життя. Вона багато вчилася і стала програмісткою. Тепер вона працює айтівкою в Києві. Вона дуже любить Україну. Вона мріє стати громадянкою України наступного року. Її чоловік теж хоче стати громадянином.
>
> *(This is Anna. Previously she lived in another country and was a simple waitress. She worked a lot, but really wanted to change her life. She studied a lot and became a programmer. Now she works as an IT professional in Kyiv. She loves Ukraine very much. She dreams of becoming a citizen of Ukraine next year. Her husband also wants to become a citizen.)*

---

# Підсумок

Congratulations! You can now confidently describe your career path and professional aspirations in Ukrainian! 

You learned that while present-tense identities use the nominative case (**Я лікар**), your past roles, future goals, and current functions all require the instrumental case (**Я був лікарем**, **Я працюю лікарем**). You also explored the modern, respectful use of femininitives and essential IT vocabulary that reflects contemporary Ukrainian society. 

Here are a few questions to check your knowledge:

1. **Як сказати українською: "I want to become a programmer"?**
   *(How do you say in Ukrainian: "I want to become a programmer"?)*
   → Я хочу стати програмістом (або програмісткою).

2. **Яка правильна форма: "Він працює як менеджер" чи "Він працює менеджером"?**
   *(Which is the correct form: "Він працює як менеджер" or "Він працює менеджером"?)*
   → Він працює менеджером.

3. **Як змінити речення "Вона лікар" на минулий час?**
   *(How do you change the sentence "Вона лікар" to the past tense?)*
   → Вона була лікаркою.

4. **Як сказати українською: "She dreams of becoming a citizen of Ukraine"?**
   *(How do you say in Ukrainian: "She dreams of becoming a citizen of Ukraine"?)*
   → Вона мріє стати громадянкою України.

# Being and Becoming

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> Today you'll learn how to talk about professions, past roles, and future goals. Being able to explain what you do, what you used to do, and what you are studying to become is essential for building relationships. By the end of this module, you'll be able to describe your career and aspirations in Ukrainian!

## Вступ (Introduction)
<!-- adapted from: Vashulenko, Grade 3, вправа 54 -->

Welcome to a very important milestone in your language journey! When you meet someone new in Ukraine, one of the first things they will ask you is about your profession. In English, we say "I am a doctor" or "I want to be a doctor." In both of these sentences, the word "doctor" stays exactly the same. However, in Ukrainian, a profession can represent two different grammatical ideas: **identity** or **function**.

When you state your permanent, present identity, you use the **називний відмінок** (nominative case). Think of this as who you are right now. This is a simple statement of fact.

- **Я лікар.** — I am a doctor.
- **Він програміст.** — He is a programmer.
- **Вона вчителька.** — She is a teacher.
- **Ми студенти.** — We are students.
- **Моя сестра лікарка.** — My sister is a doctor.
- **Твоя мама інженерка.** — Your mom is an engineer.

However, when you talk about a temporary role, a past job, or a future career goal, the Ukrainian language shifts. It uses the **орудний відмінок** (instrumental case). This case shows a process, a professional function, or a state of change rather than a fixed, permanent identity. This aligns directly with the official Ukrainian State Standard for describing a person's characteristics by profession or status.

- **Я буду лікарем.** — I will be a doctor.
- **Він хоче стати програмістом.** — He wants to become a programmer.
- **Вона працювала вчителькою.** — She worked as a teacher.
- **Ми були студентами.** — We were students.
- **Моя сестра буде лікаркою.** — My sister will be a doctor.
- **Твоя мама працювала інженеркою.** — Your mom worked as an engineer.

> [!warning] The Nominative Trap
>
> English speakers often fall into a common grammatical trap. Because English uses the same form for present and future professions, learners often want to say: ~~Він хоче бути лікар.~~ This is a direct translation from "He wants to be a doctor," but it sounds very unnatural to a Ukrainian ear! The verb **бути** (to be) in the past or future requires the instrumental case for professions. It must be: **Він хоче бути лікарем.**

Let's practice reading some short texts combining both of these forms. Notice how the endings change when we switch from present identity to past or future roles.

> **(Читання / Reading Practice)**
>
> Мене звати Антон. Зараз я студент, але я дуже люблю комп'ютери. Я хочу бути програмістом. Мій брат працює айтівцем у Києві. Раніше він теж був студентом. Наша сестра — лікарка. Вона довго була лише студенткою, а тепер працює лікаркою в лікарні. Це дуже цікаво!
>
> *(My name is Anton. Right now I am a student, but I really like computers. I want to be a programmer. My brother works as an IT professional in Kyiv. Previously he was also a student. Our sister is a doctor. She was just a student for a long time, and now she works as a doctor in a hospital. It is very interesting!)*

## Презентація: Дієслова та відмінювання (Presentation: Verbs and Governing)
<!-- adapted from: Kravtsova, Grade 4, вправа 53 -->

Now that you understand the logic behind the cases, let's look closely at the three main verbs we use to talk about our careers: **бути** (to be), **стати / ставати** (to become), and **працювати** (to work). All three of these verbs govern the **орудний відмінок** (instrumental case). This means that any profession following these verbs must take an instrumental ending.

First, let's explore the verb **бути**. While the present tense often drops this verb completely (using the nominative case with a null-copula), the past and future tenses absolutely require it, along with the instrumental case. These are some of the most high-frequency collocations you will hear in professional settings.

- **Він був студентом.** — He was a student.
- **Вона була студенткою.** — She was a student.
- **Я буду лікарем.** — I will be a doctor.
- **Ти будеш вчителем?** — Will you be a teacher?
- **Ми були друзями.** — We were friends.
- **Вони будуть інженерами.** — They will be engineers.
- **Ми будемо працювати юристами.** — We will work as lawyers.

Next, we have the verbs for "becoming": the perfective verb **стати** (to become a finished state) and the imperfective verb **ставати** (to be becoming, describing an ongoing process). The verb **стати** is extremely common when discussing career aspirations and future goals. You will often hear the construction **хоче стати...** (wants to become...).

- **Він хоче стати спеціалістом.** — He wants to become a specialist.
- **Вона стала дуже відомою журналісткою.** — She became a very famous journalist.
- **Він довго ставав кращим програмістом.** — He was becoming a better programmer for a long time.
- **Вона довго ставала хорошою вчителькою.** — She was becoming a good teacher for a long time.
- **Кожного дня ти повинен ставати кращим.** — Every day you must be becoming better.
- **Я буду хорошим юристом.** — I will be a good lawyer.
- **Ми стали студентами.** — We became students.
- **Вони хочуть стати інженерами.** — They want to become engineers.

Finally, we use the verb **працювати** (to work) to describe our current, past, or future jobs. This verb also requires the instrumental case.

> [!caution] The 'Work As' Calque
>
> In English, you say "I work *as* a manager." You might be tempted to translate the word "as" into the Ukrainian word **як**. Never say: ~~Він працює як менеджер~~! This is a translation calque. In Ukrainian, the verb **працювати** directly takes the instrumental case with no extra prepositions. You simply say: **Він працює менеджером** (He works "by means of" a manager).

- **Я працюю вчителькою.** — I work as a teacher.
- **Він працює лікарем.** — He works as a doctor.
- **Вона працює менеджеркою.** — She works as a manager.
- **Мій друг працює юристом.** — My friend works as a lawyer.
- **Ти хочеш працювати програмістом?** — Do you want to work as a programmer?

Let's introduce some primary profession vocabulary. Notice how we provide both masculine and feminine forms for each profession, alongside their instrumental endings!

- **лікар / лікарка** → лікарем / лікаркою (doctor)
- **вчитель / вчителька** → вчителем / вчителькою (teacher)
- **інженер / інженерка** → інженером / інженеркою (engineer)
- **журналіст / журналістка** → журналістом / журналісткою (journalist)
- **юрист / юристка** → юристом / юристкою (lawyer)
- **програміст / програмістка** → програмістом / програмісткою (programmer)
- **економіст / економістка** → економістом / економісткою (economist)

When discussing technology roles, you might encounter the formal word **програмувальник**. While this is perfectly correct, the word **програміст** is far more universally used in everyday speech and professional environments. Both require the instrumental case.

> **(Читання / Reading Practice)**
>
> Моя мама все життя працювала вчителькою в школі. Вона дуже любить дітей і свою роботу. Мій батько працює інженером на великому заводі. А я хочу стати журналісткою. Я вже працюю в газеті, але зараз я тільки студентка. Минулого року мій старший брат став юристом. Він працює в центрі міста.
>
> *(My mom worked as a teacher in a school her whole life. She loves children and her job very much. My father works as an engineer at a big factory. And I want to become a journalist. I already work at a newspaper, but right now I am only a student. Last year my older brother became a lawyer. He works in the city center.)*

## Соціокультурний контекст: Фемінітиви та IT (Sociocultural Context: Femininitives and IT)
<!-- adapted from: Savchenko, Grade 4, вправа 93 -->

When learning Ukrainian vocabulary for professions, it is absolutely vital to understand two major modern cultural trends: the widespread, official use of femininitives and the incredibly high prestige of the IT industry.

In 2020, the Ukrainian government passed a major Grammar Reform. For many decades, the Russian language strongly influenced Ukrainian grammar rules, making masculine forms the default for almost all official professions. The 2020 reform officially codified femininitives — words that specifically identify a female professional — returning the language to its natural, historic roots. In the past, words like **директор** (director) or **менеджер** (manager) were often used for both men and women in formal contexts. Today, using the feminine forms reflects modern societal shifts towards gender equality and respect.

- **Він хороший директор.** — He is a good director.
- **Вона дуже хороша директорка.** — She is a very good director.
- **Цей чоловік — мій менеджер.** — This man is my manager.
- **Ця жінка — моя менеджерка.** — This woman is my manager.
- **Він відомий філолог.** — He is a famous philologist.
- **Вона відома філологиня.** — She is a famous philologist.

You should practice using both masculine and feminine forms consistently. It is a clear sign of an educated, contemporary Ukrainian speaker! Using words like **директорка** and **менеджерка** is not just grammatically correct; it is culturally expected.

Another major aspect of modern Ukraine is the booming technology sector. Ukraine is known globally as a massive European IT hub, and careers in tech are highly sought after by young people. Because of this high prestige, you will often hear two different words for a tech professional. The formal word is **програміст** (or **програмістка**). However, in everyday conversation, almost everyone uses the colloquial term **айтівець** (IT professional, masculine) and **айтівка** (IT professional, feminine).

> [!culture] A Modern Dream
>
> It is a common cultural observation in Ukraine that almost every young person wants to join the IT sector because of its stability and international connections. It is very common to hear someone joke: **«Кожен другий хоче стати айтівцем!»** (Every second person wants to become an IT professional!).

- **Мій син хоче стати програмістом.** — My son wants to become a programmer.
- **Її донька працює айтівкою.** — Her daughter works as an IT professional.
- **Він працює успішним айтівцем.** — He works as a successful IT professional.

> **(Читання / Reading Practice)**
>
> Сучасна Україна дуже швидко змінюється. Сьогодні багато жінок працюють директорками та менеджерками. Також дуже популярна сфера ІТ. Кожен другий студент хоче працювати айтівцем або програмістом. Моя подруга Олена стала дуже хорошою програмісткою. Вона працює айтівкою у великій міжнародній компанії. Її мама теж багато працює — вона працює директоркою школи.
>
> *(Modern Ukraine is changing very quickly. Today many women work as directors and managers. The IT sphere is also very popular. Every second student wants to work as an IT professional or a programmer. My friend Olena became a very good programmer. She works as an IT professional in a large international company. Her mom also works a lot — she works as a school director.)*

## Практика та запобігання помилкам (Practice and Error Prevention)
<!-- adapted from: Varzatska, Grade 4, вправа 237 -->

Let's actively practice transforming present identities into professional roles and work on correcting common learner mistakes! 

When changing a present-tense identity into a past or future role, you must change the ending of the noun to the **орудний відмінок** (instrumental case). Watch carefully how the endings change for masculine nouns (usually adding **-ом** or **-ем**) and feminine nouns (usually adding **-ою** or **-ею**).

- **Я економіст.** → **Я був економістом.** (I was an economist.)
- **Ти юристка.** → **Ти будеш юристкою.** (You will be a lawyer.)
- **Він журналіст.** → **Він став журналістом.** (He became a journalist.)
- **Вона інженерка.** → **Вона стала головною інженеркою.** (She became a chief engineer.)
- **Я спеціаліст.** → **Я хочу стати хорошим спеціалістом.** (I want to become a good specialist.)
- **Ти лікар.** → **Ти будеш працювати лікарем.** (You will work as a doctor.)

> [!tip] Gender Mismatch Warning
>
> When talking about a woman's profession, you must ensure that both the adjective and the noun match her gender! English speakers sometimes default to the masculine form because English does not have gendered professions. Avoid saying: ~~Вона хороший лікар.~~
> Instead, correctly drill the agreement between the gendered person noun and the profession: **Вона хороша лікарка.**

Let's drill some gender-matching phrases. Note how the instrumental case affects both the adjective and the noun simultaneously!

- **Він був хорошим лікарем.** — He was a good doctor.
- **Вона була дуже хорошою лікаркою.** — She was a very good doctor.
- **Він хоче стати новим директором.** — He wants to become the new director.
- **Вона хоче стати новою директоркою.** — She wants to become the new director.
- **Він працює головним інженером.** — He works as a chief engineer.
- **Вона працює головною інженеркою.** — She works as a chief engineer.

Finally, we must practice removing the translation calque when using the verb **працювати** (to work). Exercises specifically designed to root out the use of the word **як** (as) are crucial for sounding natural. Remember, the instrumental case alone provides the meaning of "as."

- ~~Він працює як економіст.~~ → **Він працює економістом.** (He works as an economist.)
- ~~Вона працює як спеціаліст.~~ → **Вона працює спеціалісткою.** (She works as a specialist.)
- ~~Я працював як журналіст.~~ → **Я працював журналістом.** (I worked as a journalist.)

> **(Читання / Reading Practice)**
>
> Раніше мій дідусь був інженером. Він багато років працював головним інженером на великому заводі. Моя бабуся була дуже відомою економісткою. Вона ніколи не працювала як журналістка чи вчителька. Вона завжди працювала економісткою в банку. Зараз я студент, але я мрію стати відомим спеціалістом. Моя молодша сестра хоче стати юристкою.
>
> *(Previously my grandpa was an engineer. He worked as a chief engineer at a big factory for many years. My grandma was a famous economist. She never worked as a journalist or a teacher. She always worked as an economist in a bank. Right now I am a student, but I dream of becoming a famous specialist. My younger sister wants to become a lawyer.)*

## Діалоги та кар'єрні плани (Dialogues and Career Plans)
<!-- adapted from: Kravtsova, Grade 3, вправа 313 -->

The most common way to ask someone about their job in Ukrainian is **«Ким ви працюєте?»** (Who do you work as?) in formal situations, or **«Ким ти працюєш?»** in informal situations. Notice that the question word **хто** (who) changes to its instrumental form **ким** (by whom). 

Let's look at some natural conversations about careers, past roles, and future goals. These dialogues model natural modern usage in IT and professional contexts. Read them aloud to practice your pronunciation!

> **(В офісі / In the office)**
> — Добрий день! Ви тут новий співробітник? Ким ви працюєте?
> — Добрий день! Так. Я працюю менеджером. А ви?
> — Дуже приємно! А я працюю головною директоркою тут.
> — О, це цікаво. Я раніше працював інженером.
>
> *(— Good afternoon! Are you a new employee? Who do you work as?)*
> *(— Good afternoon! Yes. I work as a manager. And you?)*
> *(— Nice to meet you! And I work as the chief director here.)*
> *(— Oh, that is interesting. I previously worked as an engineer.)*

> **(На вулиці / On the street)**
> — Привіт, Олеже! Давно не бачилися! Ким ти зараз працюєш?
> — Привіт! Я став програмістом. Працюю айтівцем у новій компанії.
> — Клас! А я ще студент. Хочу стати юристом.
> — Бажаю успіху! Це хороша професія.
>
> *(— Hi, Oleh! Long time no see! Who do you work as now?)*
> *(— Hi! I became a programmer. I work as an IT professional in a new company.)*
> *(— Cool! And I am still a student. I want to become a lawyer.)*
> *(— I wish you success! It is a good profession.)*

We also use these exact same verbs and cases to talk about citizenship and broader life aspirations. In the official Ukrainian State Standard for language learning, there is a beautiful example sentence that reflects the goals of many people learning the language today: **Вона мріє стати громадянкою України.** (She dreams of becoming a citizen of Ukraine.)

- **Він став громадянином.** — He became a citizen.
- **Вона хоче стати громадянкою.** — She wants to become a citizen.
- **Я мрію бути громадянином України.** — I dream of being a citizen of Ukraine.
- **Ти будеш хорошим громадянином.** — You will be a good citizen.

> [!did-you-know] Synthesis: Your Career Story
>
> You can now describe your entire career path! Practice writing down your own story using the full range of vocabulary introduced today: write what you were in the past (**був / була**), what you work as now (**працюю**), and what you want to become in the future (**хочу стати**). 

> **(Читання / Reading Practice)**
>
> Це Анна. Раніше вона жила в іншій країні і працювала звичайною офіціанткою. Вона багато працювала, але дуже хотіла змінити своє життя. Вона багато вчилася і стала хорошою програмісткою. Тепер вона працює айтівкою в Києві. Вона дуже любить Україну. Вона мріє наступного року стати громадянкою України. Її чоловік теж хоче стати громадянином.
>
> *(This is Anna. Previously she lived in another country and was a simple waitress. She worked a lot, but really wanted to change her life. She studied a lot and became a programmer. Now she works as an IT professional in Kyiv. She loves Ukraine very much. She dreams of becoming a citizen of Ukraine next year. Her husband also wants to become a citizen.)*

---

# Підсумок

Congratulations! You can now confidently describe your career path and professional aspirations in Ukrainian! 

You learned that while present-tense identities use the nominative case (**Я лікар**), your past roles, future goals, and current functions all require the instrumental case (**Я був лікарем**, **Я працюю лікарем**). You also explored the modern, respectful use of femininitives and essential IT vocabulary that reflects contemporary Ukrainian society. 

Here are a few questions to check your knowledge:

1. **Як сказати українською: "I want to become a programmer"?**
   *(How do you say in Ukrainian: "I want to become a programmer"?)*
   → Я хочу стати програмістом (або програмісткою).

2. **Яка правильна форма: "Він працює як менеджер" чи "Він працює менеджером"?**
   *(Which is the correct form: "Він працює як менеджер" or "Він працює менеджером"?)*
   → Він працює менеджером.

3. **Як змінити речення "Вона лікар" на минулий час?**
   *(How do you change the sentence "Вона лікар" to the past tense?)*
   → Вона була лікаркою.

4. **Як сказати українською: "She dreams of becoming a citizen of Ukraine"?**
   *(How do you say in Ukrainian: "She dreams of becoming a citizen of Ukraine"?)*
   → Вона мріє стати громадянкою України.

---

resultDisplay.originalContent:
<!-- SCOPE
Covers: Professions, instrumental case with verbs бути, стати, працювати
Not covered:
  - Complex instrumental prepositions (з, над, під) → a2-07
-->

# Being and Becoming

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> Today you'll learn how to talk about professions, past roles, and future goals. Being able to explain what you do, what you used to do, and what you are studying to become is essential for building relationships. By the end of this module, you'll be able to describe your career and aspirations in Ukrainian!

## Вступ (Introduction)
<!-- adapted from: Vashulenko, Grade 3, вправа 54 -->

Welcome to a very important milestone in your language journey! When you meet someone new in Ukraine, one of the first things they will ask you is about your profession. In English, we say "I am a doctor" or "I want to be a doctor." In both of these sentences, the word "doctor" stays exactly the same. However, in Ukrainian, a profession can represent two different grammatical ideas: **identity** or **function**.

When you state your permanent, present identity, you use the **називний відмінок** (nominative case). Think of this as who you are right now. This is a simple statement of fact.

- **Я лікар.** — I am a doctor.
- **Він програміст.** — He is a programmer.
- **Вона вчителька.** — She is a teacher.
- **Ми студенти.** — We are students.
- **Моя сестра лікарка.** — My sister is a doctor.
- **Твоя мама інженерка.** — Your mom is an engineer.

However, when you talk about a temporary role, a past job, or a future career goal, the Ukrainian language shifts. It uses the **орудний відмінок** (instrumental case). This case shows a process, a professional function, or a state of change rather than a fixed, permanent identity. This aligns directly with the official Ukrainian State Standard for describing a person's characteristics by profession or status.

- **Я буду лікарем.** — I will be a doctor.
- **Він хоче стати програмістом.** — He wants to become a programmer.
- **Вона працювала вчителькою.** — She worked as a teacher.
- **Ми були студентами.** — We were students.
- **Моя сестра буде лікаркою.** — My sister will be a doctor.
- **Твоя мама працювала інженеркою.** — Your mom worked as an engineer.

> [!warning] The Nominative Trap
>
> English speakers often fall into a common grammatical trap. Because English uses the same form for present and future professions, learners often want to say: ~~Він хоче бути лікар.~~ This is a direct translation from "He wants to be a doctor," but it sounds very unnatural to a Ukrainian ear! The verb **бути** (to be) in the past or future requires the instrumental case for professions. It must be: **Він хоче бути лікарем.**

Let's practice reading some short texts combining both of these forms. Notice how the endings change when we switch from present identity to past or future roles.

> **(Читання / Reading Practice)**
>
> Мене звати Антон. Зараз я студент, але я дуже люблю комп'ютери. Я хочу бути програмістом. Мій брат працює айтівцем у Києві. Раніше він теж був студентом. Наша сестра — лікарка. Вона довго була лише студенткою, а тепер працює лікаркою в лікарні. Це дуже цікаво!
>
> *(My name is Anton. Right now I am a student, but I really like computers. I want to be a programmer. My brother works as an IT professional in Kyiv. Previously he was also a student. Our sister is a doctor. She was just a student for a long time, and now she works as a doctor in a hospital. It is very interesting!)*

## Презентація: Дієслова та відмінювання (Presentation: Verbs and Governing)
<!-- adapted from: Kravtsova, Grade 4, вправа 53 -->

Now that you understand the logic behind the cases, let's look closely at the three main verbs we use to talk about our careers: **бути** (to be), **стати / ставати** (to become), and **працювати** (to work). All three of these verbs govern the **орудний відмінок** (instrumental case). This means that any profession following these verbs must take an instrumental ending.

First, let's explore the verb **бути**. While the present tense often drops this verb completely (using the nominative case with a null-copula), the past and future tenses absolutely require it, along with the instrumental case. These are some of the most high-frequency collocations you will hear in professional settings.

- **Він був студентом.** — He was a student.
- **Вона була студенткою.** — She was a student.
- **Я буду лікарем.** — I will be a doctor.
- **Ти будеш вчителем?** — Will you be a teacher?
- **Ми були друзями.** — We were friends.
- **Вони будуть інженерами.** — They will be engineers.
- **Ми будемо працювати юристами.** — We will work as lawyers.

Next, we have the verbs for "becoming": the perfective verb **стати** (to become a finished state) and the imperfective verb **ставати** (to be becoming, describing an ongoing process). The verb **стати** is extremely common when discussing career aspirations and future goals. You will often hear the construction **хоче стати...** (wants to become...).

- **Він хоче стати спеціалістом.** — He wants to become a specialist.
- **Вона стала дуже відомою журналісткою.** — She became a very famous journalist.
- **Він довго ставав кращим програмістом.** — He was becoming a better programmer for a long time.
- **Вона довго ставала хорошою вчителькою.** — She was becoming a good teacher for a long time.
- **Кожного дня ти повинен ставати кращим.** — Every day you must be becoming better.
- **Я буду хорошим юристом.** — I will be a good lawyer.
- **Ми стали студентами.** — We became students.
- **Вони хочуть стати інженерами.** — They want to become engineers.

Finally, we use the verb **працювати** (to work) to describe our current, past, or future jobs. This verb also requires the instrumental case.

> [!caution] The 'Work As' Calque
>
> In English, you say "I work *as* a manager." You might be tempted to translate the word "as" into the Ukrainian word **як**. Never say: ~~Він працює як менеджер~~! This is a translation calque. In Ukrainian, the verb **працювати** directly takes the instrumental case with no extra prepositions. You simply say: **Він працює менеджером** (He works "by means of" a manager).

- **Я працюю вчителькою.** — I work as a teacher.
- **Він працює лікарем.** — He works as a doctor.
- **Вона працює менеджеркою.** — She works as a manager.
- **Мій друг працює юристом.** — My friend works as a lawyer.
- **Ти хочеш працювати програмістом?** — Do you want to work as a programmer?

Let's introduce some primary profession vocabulary. Notice how we provide both masculine and feminine forms for each profession, alongside their instrumental endings!

- **лікар / лікарка** → лікарем / лікаркою (doctor)
- **вчитель / вчителька** → вчителем / вчителькою (teacher)
- **інженер / інженерка** → інженером / інженеркою (engineer)
- **журналіст / журналістка** → журналістом / журналісткою (journalist)
- **юрист / юристка** → юристом / юристкою (lawyer)
- **програміст / програмістка** → програмістом / програмісткою (programmer)
- **економіст / економістка** → економістом / економісткою (economist)

When discussing technology roles, you might encounter the formal word **програмувальник**. While this is perfectly correct, the word **програміст** is far more universally used in everyday speech and professional environments. Both require the instrumental case.

> **(Читання / Reading Practice)**
>
> Моя мама все життя працювала вчителькою в школі. Вона дуже любить дітей і свою роботу. Мій батько працює інженером на великому заводі. А я хочу стати журналісткою. Я вже працюю в газеті, але зараз я тільки студентка. Минулого року мій старший брат став юристом. Він працює в центрі міста.
>
> *(My mom worked as a teacher in a school her whole life. She loves children and her job very much. My father works as an engineer at a big factory. And I want to become a journalist. I already work at a newspaper, but right now I am only a student. Last year my older brother became a lawyer. He works in the city center.)*

## Соціокультурний контекст: Фемінітиви та IT (Sociocultural Context: Femininitives and IT)
<!-- adapted from: Savchenko, Grade 4, вправа 93 -->

When learning Ukrainian vocabulary for professions, it is absolutely vital to understand two major modern cultural trends: the widespread, official use of femininitives and the incredibly high prestige of the IT industry.

In 2020, the Ukrainian government passed a major Grammar Reform. For many decades, the Russian language strongly influenced Ukrainian grammar rules, making masculine forms the default for almost all official professions. The 2020 reform officially codified femininitives — words that specifically identify a female professional — returning the language to its natural, historic roots. In the past, words like **директор** (director) or **менеджер** (manager) were often used for both men and women in formal contexts. Today, using the feminine forms reflects modern societal shifts towards gender equality and respect.

- **Він хороший директор.** — He is a good director.
- **Вона дуже хороша директорка.** — She is a very good director.
- **Цей чоловік — мій менеджер.** — This man is my manager.
- **Ця жінка — моя менеджерка.** — This woman is my manager.
- **Він відомий філолог.** — He is a famous philologist.
- **Вона відома філологиня.** — She is a famous philologist.

You should practice using both masculine and feminine forms consistently. It is a clear sign of an educated, contemporary Ukrainian speaker! Using words like **директорка** and **менеджерка** is not just grammatically correct; it is culturally expected.

Another major aspect of modern Ukraine is the booming technology sector. Ukraine is known globally as a massive European IT hub, and careers in tech are highly sought after by young people. Because of this high prestige, you will often hear two different words for a tech professional. The formal word is **програміст** (or **програмістка**). However, in everyday conversation, almost everyone uses the colloquial term **айтівець** (IT professional, masculine) and **айтівка** (IT professional, feminine).

> [!culture] A Modern Dream
>
> It is a common cultural observation in Ukraine that almost every young person wants to join the IT sector because of its stability and international connections. It is very common to hear someone joke: **«Кожен другий хоче стати айтівцем!»** (Every second person wants to become an IT professional!).

- **Мій син хоче стати програмістом.** — My son wants to become a programmer.
- **Її донька працює айтівкою.** — Her daughter works as an IT professional.
- **Він працює успішним айтівцем.** — He works as a successful IT professional.

> **(Читання / Reading Practice)**
>
> Сучасна Україна дуже швидко змінюється. Сьогодні багато жінок працюють директорками та менеджерками. Також дуже популярна сфера ІТ. Кожен другий студент хоче працювати айтівцем або програмістом. Моя подруга Олена стала дуже хорошою програмісткою. Вона працює айтівкою у великій міжнародній компанії. Її мама теж багато працює — вона працює директоркою школи.
>
> *(Modern Ukraine is changing very quickly. Today many women work as directors and managers. The IT sphere is also very popular. Every second student wants to work as an IT professional or a programmer. My friend Olena became a very good programmer. She works as an IT professional in a large international company. Her mom also works a lot — she works as a school director.)*

## Практика та запобігання помилкам (Practice and Error Prevention)
<!-- adapted from: Varzatska, Grade 4, вправа 237 -->

Let's actively practice transforming present identities into professional roles and work on correcting common learner mistakes! 

When changing a present-tense identity into a past or future role, you must change the ending of the noun to the **орудний відмінок** (instrumental case). Watch carefully how the endings change for masculine nouns (usually adding **-ом** or **-ем**) and feminine nouns (usually adding **-ою** or **-ею**).

- **Я економіст.** → **Я був економістом.** (I was an economist.)
- **Ти юристка.** → **Ти будеш юристкою.** (You will be a lawyer.)
- **Він журналіст.** → **Він став журналістом.** (He became a journalist.)
- **Вона інженерка.** → **Вона стала головною інженеркою.** (She became a chief engineer.)
- **Я спеціаліст.** → **Я хочу стати хорошим спеціалістом.** (I want to become a good specialist.)
- **Ти лікар.** → **Ти будеш працювати лікарем.** (You will work as a doctor.)

> [!tip] Gender Mismatch Warning
>
> When talking about a woman's profession, you must ensure that both the adjective and the noun match her gender! English speakers sometimes default to the masculine form because English does not have gendered professions. Avoid saying: ~~Вона хороший лікар.~~
> Instead, correctly drill the agreement between the gendered person noun and the profession: **Вона хороша лікарка.**

Let's drill some gender-matching phrases. Note how the instrumental case affects both the adjective and the noun simultaneously!

- **Він був хорошим лікарем.** — He was a good doctor.
- **Вона була дуже хорошою лікаркою.** — She was a very good doctor.
- **Він хоче стати новим директором.** — He wants to become the new director.
- **Вона хоче стати новою директоркою.** — She wants to become the new director.
- **Він працює головним інженером.** — He works as a chief engineer.
- **Вона працює головною інженеркою.** — She works as a chief engineer.

Finally, we must practice removing the translation calque when using the verb **працювати** (to work). Exercises specifically designed to root out the use of the word **як** (as) are crucial for sounding natural. Remember, the instrumental case alone provides the meaning of "as."

- ~~Він працює як економіст.~~ → **Він працює економістом.** (He works as an economist.)
- ~~Вона працює як спеціаліст.~~ → **Вона працює спеціалісткою.** (She works as a specialist.)
- ~~Я працював як журналіст.~~ → **Я працював журналістом.** (I worked as a journalist.)

> **(Читання / Reading Practice)**
>
> Раніше мій дідусь був інженером. Він багато років працював головним інженером на великому заводі. Моя бабуся була дуже відомою економісткою. Вона ніколи не працювала як журналістка чи вчителька. Вона завжди працювала економісткою в банку. Зараз я студент, але я мрію стати відомим спеціалістом. Моя молодша сестра хоче стати юристкою.
>
> *(Previously my grandpa was an engineer. He worked as a chief engineer at a big factory for many years. My grandma was a famous economist. She never worked as a journalist or a teacher. She always worked as an economist in a bank. Right now I am a student, but I dream of becoming a famous specialist. My younger sister wants to become a lawyer.)*

## Діалоги та кар'єрні плани (Dialogues and Career Plans)
<!-- adapted from: Kravtsova, Grade 3, вправа 313 -->

The most common way to ask someone about their job in Ukrainian is **«Ким ви працюєте?»** (Who do you work as?) in formal situations, or **«Ким ти працюєш?»** in informal situations. Notice that the question word **хто** (who) changes to its instrumental form **ким** (by whom). 

Let's look at some natural conversations about careers, past roles, and future goals. These dialogues model natural modern usage in IT and professional contexts. Read them aloud to practice your pronunciation!

> **(В офісі / In the office)**
> — Добрий день! Ви тут новий співробітник? Ким ви працюєте?
> — Добрий день! Так. Я працюю менеджером. А ви?
> — Дуже приємно! А я працюю головною директоркою тут.
> — О, це цікаво. Я раніше працював інженером.
>
> *(— Good afternoon! Are you a new employee? Who do you work as?)*
> *(— Good afternoon! Yes. I work as a manager. And you?)*
> *(— Nice to meet you! And I work as the chief director here.)*
> *(— Oh, that is interesting. I previously worked as an engineer.)*

> **(На вулиці / On the street)**
> — Привіт, Олеже! Давно не бачилися! Ким ти зараз працюєш?
> — Привіт! Я став програмістом. Працюю айтівцем у новій компанії.
> — Клас! А я ще студент. Хочу стати юристом.
> — Бажаю успіху! Це хороша професія.
>
> *(— Hi, Oleh! Long time no see! Who do you work as now?)*
> *(— Hi! I became a programmer. I work as an IT professional in a new company.)*
> *(— Cool! And I am still a student. I want to become a lawyer.)*
> *(— I wish you success! It is a good profession.)*

We also use these exact same verbs and cases to talk about citizenship and broader life aspirations. In the official Ukrainian State Standard for language learning, there is a beautiful example sentence that reflects the goals of many people learning the language today: **Вона мріє стати громадянкою України.** (She dreams of becoming a citizen of Ukraine.)

- **Він став громадянином.** — He became a citizen.
- **Вона хоче стати громадянкою.** — She wants to become a citizen.
- **Я мрію бути громадянином України.** — I dream of being a citizen of Ukraine.
- **Ти будеш хорошим громадянином.** — You will be a good citizen.

> [!did-you-know] Synthesis: Your Career Story
>
> You can now describe your entire career path! Practice writing down your own story using the full range of vocabulary introduced today: write what you were in the past (**був / була**), what you work as now (**працюю**), and what you want to become in the future (**хочу стати**). 

> **(Читання / Reading Practice)**
>
> Це Анна. Раніше вона жила в іншій країні і працювала звичайною офіціанткою. Вона багато працювала, але дуже хотіла змінити своє життя. Вона багато вчилася і стала хорошою програмісткою. Тепер вона працює айтівкою в Києві. Вона дуже любить Україну. Вона мріє наступного року стати громадянкою України. Її чоловік теж хоче стати громадянином.
>
> *(This is Anna. Previously she lived in another country and was a simple waitress. She worked a lot, but really wanted to change her life. She studied a lot and became a programmer. Now she works as an IT professional in Kyiv. She loves Ukraine very much. She dreams of becoming a citizen of Ukraine next year. Her husband also wants to become a citizen.)*

---

# Підсумок

Congratulations! You can now confidently describe your career path and professional aspirations in Ukrainian! 

You learned that while present-tense identities use the nominative case (**Я лікар**), your past roles, future goals, and current functions all require the instrumental case (**Я був лікарем**, **Я працюю лікарем**). You also explored the modern, respectful use of femininitives and essential IT vocabulary that reflects contemporary Ukrainian society. 

Here are a few questions to check your knowledge:

1. **Як сказати українською: "I want to become a programmer"?**
   *(How do you say in Ukrainian: "I want to become a programmer"?)*
   → Я хочу стати програмістом (або програмісткою).

2. **Яка правильна форма: "Він працює як менеджер" чи "Він працює менеджером"?**
   *(Which is the correct form: "Він працює як менеджер" or "Він працює менеджером"?)*
   → Він працює менеджером.

3. **Як змінити речення "Вона лікар" на минулий час?**
   *(How do you change the sentence "Вона лікар" to the past tense?)*
   → Вона була лікаркою.

4. **Як сказати українською: "She dreams of becoming a citizen of Ukraine"?**
   *(How do you say in Ukrainian: "She dreams of becoming a citizen of Ukraine"?)*
   → Вона мріє стати громадянкою України.
